corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: By conditioning an autoregressive model on the desired return (reward), past states, and actions, our Decision Transformer model can gen- erate future actions that achieve the desired return.

context_window.previous: Un- like prior approaches to RL that fit value functions or compute policy gradients, Decision Transformer simply outputs the optimal actions by leveraging a causally masked Transformer.
context_window.current: By conditioning an autoregressive model on the desired return (reward), past states, and actions, our Decision Transformer model can gen- erate future actions that achieve the desired return.
context_window.next: Despite its simplicity, Decision Transformer matches or exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art model-free offline RL baselines on Atari, OpenAI Gym, and Key-to-Door tasks.

location.sentence: In contrast to prior work using transformers as an architectural choice for components within traditional RL algorithms [ 4 , 5 ], we seek to study if generative trajectory modeling – i.e. modeling the joint distribution of the sequence of states, actions, and rewards – can serve as a replacement for conventional RL algorithms.

context_window.previous: Given the diversity of successful applications of such models, we seek to examine their application to sequential decision making problems formalized as reinforcement learning (RL).
context_window.current: In contrast to prior work using transformers as an architectural choice for components within traditional RL algorithms [ 4 , 5 ], we seek to study if generative trajectory modeling – i.e. modeling the joint distribution of the sequence of states, actions, and rewards – can serve as a replacement for conventional RL algorithms.
context_window.next: We consider the following shift in paradigm: instead of training a policy through conventional RL algorithms like temporal difference (TD) learning [ 6 ], we will train transformer models on collected experience using a sequence modeling objective.

top_candidates.sentence: A state-of-the-art model-free method is Conservative Q-Learning (CQL) which serves as our primary comparison.
Transformer models can also be used to model the state evolution of trajectory, potentially serving as an alternative to model-based RL, and we hope to explore this in future work.
In this section, we present Decision Transformer, which models trajectories autoregressively with minimal modification to the transformer architecture, as summarized in Figure and Algorithm 1.
In this section, we present Decision Transformer, which models trajectories autoregressively with minimal modification to the transformer architecture, as summarized in Figure and Algorithm 1.
In this section, we present Decision Transformer, which models trajectories autoregressively with minimal modification to the transformer architecture, as summarized in Figure and Algorithm 1.
Parisotto et al. discussed design decisions for more stable training of transformers in the high-variance RL setting.
Unlike our work, these still use actor-critic algorithms for optimization, focusing on novelty in architecture.
We introduce a framework that abstracts Reinforcement Learning (RL) as a sequence modeling problem.
We introduce a framework that abstracts Reinforcement Learning (RL) as a sequence modeling problem.
This allows us to draw upon the simplicity and scalability of the Transformer architecture, and associated advances in language modeling such as GPT-x and BERT.
In particular, we present Decision Transformer, an architecture that casts the problem of RL as conditional sequence modeling.
In particular, we present Decision Transformer, an architecture that casts the problem of RL as conditional sequence modeling.
Despite its simplicity, Decision Transformer matches or exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art model-free offline RL baselines on Atari, OpenAI Gym, and Key-to-Door tasks.
Despite its simplicity, Decision Transformer matches or exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art model-free offline RL baselines on Atari, OpenAI Gym, and Key-to-Door tasks.

Furthermore, on some Atari tasks like Seaquest, we can prompt the Decision Transformer with higher returns than the maximum episode return available in the dataset, demonstrating that Decision Transformer is sometimes capable of extrapolation.

In particular, our primary points of comparison are modelfree offline RL algorithms based on TD-learning, since our Decision Transformer architecture is fundamentally model-free in nature as well.

On standard offline RL benchmarks, we showed Decision Transformer can match or outperform strong algorithms designed explicitly for offline RL with minimal modifications from standard language modeling architectures.
For instance, reward design by nefarious actors can potentially generate unintended behaviors as our model generates behaviors by conditioning on desired returns.
We train on a dataset of 1, 000 graph random walk trajectories of T = 10 steps each with a random graph of 20 nodes and edge sparsity coefficient of 0.1.
In one case, the training dataset of random walk trajectories may contain a segment that directly corresponds to the desired shortest path, in which case it will be generated by the model.
In the second case, generated paths are entirely original and are not subsets of trajectories in the training dataset -they are generated from stitching sub-optimal segments.
